metadata.summary: Spring Boot 3.x layered architecture for REST APIs: @RestController → @Service → JpaRepository with Pageable, @Async with named executor beans, MapStruct DTO mapping, Bean Validation, and slice tests.

content.title: Java Spring (Layered Architecture)
content.summary: Spring Boot 3.x layered architecture for REST APIs: @RestController → @Service → JpaRepository with Pageable, @Async with named executor beans, MapStruct DTO mapping, Bean Validation, and slice tests (@WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest). Covers both the MVC/JPA stack and Spring Async patterns.

why.p: The layered pattern is the dominant Spring Boot idiom, but it produces subtle runtime bugs — lazy-load exceptions after transaction close, @Async swallowing exceptions in void returns, @Transactional on self-invoked methods doing nothing (proxy bypass), and findAll() without Pageable OOMing prod. This methodology encodes the guardrails so agents produce correct, production-safe code on the first pass.

list.item: Greenfield REST APIs in Spring Boot 3.x, Java 17+, using JPA/Hibernate and constructor injection.
Adding async work via @Async with named ThreadPoolTaskExecutor beans per workload.
Migration from Spring Boot 2.x / Java EE — the patterns translate cleanly.
Full vertical slices: entity, repo, service, controller, mapper, DTOs, tests, Flyway migration in one diff.
Reactive workloads — Spring WebFlux + R2DBC; servlet patterns deadlock in reactive context.
Lightweight microservices where JVM boot time / memory footprint hurts — Quarkus, Micronaut.
Functions/FaaS — cold start; use GraalVM native image with explicit config.
CQRS/event-sourced systems — service+JPA pattern fights write/read separation.

section.name: layer-boundaries
section.p: Layer responsibilities, transaction rules, and DTO mapping conventions for the standard Spring Boot MVC/JPA stack.

rule.statement: Controllers accept HTTP requests, call one service method, and return responses. No @Transactional, no repository access, no business logic.
rule.rationale: Controllers are HTTP adapters. Business logic in controllers cannot be tested without loading the Servlet container.

rule.statement: Service methods that mutate state carry @Transactional. Read-only service methods carry @Transactional(readOnly = true) or the class-level default is readOnly = true overridden per write method.
rule.rationale: readOnly = true skips flush and enables read-replica routing. Explicit write transactions prevent silent dirty-writes.

rule.statement: Never return JPA entities from service public methods. Map to a DTO at the service boundary using MapStruct.
rule.rationale: Returning entities exposes schema internals, risks lazy-load exceptions, and prevents computed fields.

rule.statement: DTOs are Java records (Java 17+). Request records carry Bean Validation annotations. Response records carry no logic.
rule.rationale: Records are immutable, concise, and equal by value — ideal for DTO transfer objects.

section.name: mapstruct-and-lombok
section.p: Annotation processor ordering and dependency management for MapStruct + Lombok integration.

rule.statement: In maven-compiler-plugin annotationProcessorPaths, list lombok first, then mapstruct-processor, then lombok-mapstruct-binding. Never reverse the order.
rule.rationale: Lombok generates setters that MapStruct reads. If MapStruct runs before Lombok, it reads no setters and produces null mappers at runtime.

section.reference: Maven compiler plugin snippet enforcing correct annotation processor order

section.name: pagination
section.p: Preventing out-of-memory errors by requiring pagination on list endpoints.

rule.statement: Every list endpoint accepts org.springframework.data.domain.Pageable and returns Page<T>. Never expose findAll() without Pageable in a controller.
rule.rationale: findAll() without pagination loads the entire table into the JVM heap, causing OOM in production.

section.name: validation-and-error-handling
section.p: Standard validation and error response patterns.

rule.statement: Use @Valid on @RequestBody parameters. Use @RestControllerAdvice with ProblemDetail (RFC 7807) for all error responses. Never create custom error envelope classes.
rule.rationale: RFC 7807 is the industry standard for HTTP problem details. Custom envelopes diverge per endpoint and break client SDKs.

example.title: Controller with pagination and validation
example.note: No @Transactional, no repository, no business logic. Pageable on list endpoint.

example.title: Service with transactions and DTO mapping
WebMvcTest slice

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Common pitfalls when implementing the layered architecture.

antipattern.description: @Transactional on a controller method or on a private service method.
antipattern.reason: Spring AOP proxy cannot intercept controller-layer calls or private methods — the annotation is silently ignored.

antipattern.description: Self-invocation of @Async method: calling this.sendEmail() from inside the same bean.
antipattern.reason: The call bypasses the AOP proxy and runs synchronously on the calling thread, ignoring the @Async directive.

antipattern.description: Service returns JPA entity instead of DTO.
antipattern.reason: Exposes schema internals, triggers lazy-load exceptions outside @Transactional, and prevents circular-reference handling.

template.purpose: Maven compiler plugin snippet for Lombok + MapStruct in correct dependency order
